title: Undo the last commit but keep your changes

content.problemStatement:
Sometimes you make a commit and then realise: **“You committed too early!”** or \
**“I need to fix the message or add more changes.”**

In this case, you don’t want to lose your code — you only want to **remove the commit**, not your work.

---

**Git Command:**
```sss
git reset --soft HEAD~1
```

---

**What this command does**:
- Removes the latest commit from history.
- Moves all the commit’s changes back to the staging area.
- Your code is completely safe — **nothing is deleted**.
- In simple terms, this command **undoes your last commit without losing any work**.

**Breakdown of the command**:
- `git reset` → go back to an earlier commit
- `--soft` → keep all changes staged (safe)
- `HEAD` → your current commit
- `~1` → one commit back
